content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF
GREGORY D. THATCH
17301 Street, Suite 220
[illegible] SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
Telephone (916) 443-6956
Facsimile (916) 443-6632

GREGORY D. THATCH
LARRY C. LARSEN
MICHAEL DEVEREAUX

WASHINGTON, DC OFFICE
1233 20th Street NW, Suite 501
WASHINGTON, DC 20036
Telephone (202) 828-9612
Facsimile (202) 828-9614

DELBERT L. SPURLOCK, JR.

February 13, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request For Opinion

Dear Members of the Commission:

This office serves as legal counsel to the Sacramento Employment and Training Agency
(SETA), a Joint Powers Agency of the City of Sacramento and the County of Sacramento. The
SETA Governing Board (Board) has asked this office to solicit an opinion from the Commission,
pursuant to Government Code §31 l(a) and California Code of Regulation Title 2, §18320,
regarding the proper procedure to be followed by members of the Private Industry Council (PIC)
when voting on procurement activities and allocation of Job Training Partnership Act (JTPA)
funds to subcontractors of SETA. In order that you may fully understand the issues, we provide
the following background information.

BACKGROUND FACTS.

A. Job Training Partnership Act.

In 1982, the federal government enacted the JTPA "to establish programs to prepare
youth and unskilled adults for entry into the labor force and to afford job training to those
economically disadvantaged individuals and other individuals facing serious barriers to
employment, who are in special need of such training to obtain productive employment." 29
U.S.C. §1501. To accomplish these goals, the legislation provided that each state would be
divided into appropriate Service Delivery Areas (SDAs) "to promote effective delivery of job
training services" and to be "consistent with areas in which related services are provided under
other State or Federal programs." 29 U.S.C. §1511. As such, an SDA could be comprised of
"any consortium of contiguous units of local government with an aggregate population of
200,000 or more which serves a substantial part of a labor market area". Id.

1. Sacramento Employment and Training Agency.

Pursuant to the Joint Powers Agreement between the City of Sacramento and the
County of Sacramento, the SDA for the City and the County is SETA. SETA is operated under
the control and supervision of the Board, comprised of two members of the Sacramento County
Board of Supervisors, two members of the City Council of the City of Sacramento and one

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 13, 1992
Page 2

public member. In addition to serving as the local SDA under the JTPA, SETA also administers
funds under other federal and state programs such as Head Start, the Community Service Block
Grant Act, Greater Avenues for Independence, the Refugee Targeted Assistance Program and
the Stewart B. McKinney Homeless Act.

2. Private Industry Council.

The JTPA further provided for the creation, in each SDA, of a PIC, appointed
by the local SDA and comprised of "representatives of the private sector" and "representatives
of educational agencies, organized labor, rehabilitation agencies, community-based organizations,
economic development agencies and the public employment service." 29 U.S.C. §1512.
Consistent with the requirements of the JTPA, the majority of the PIC members are from the
private sector and the Chairperson of the Board is selected from among the private sector
members. In recognition of the quasi-governmental status of PICs, it has previously been
determined by the Attorney General's office that members of the PIC are public officials who
must comply with the conflict of interest provisions of the Government Code (65 Ops. Att. Gen.
41, 1-21-82). Subsequent to the Attorney General opinion, however, the State legislature, in
1985, enacted Government Code §1091.2, which excludes members of private industry councils
from the prohibitions of Government Code §1090, provided that the members comply with the
provisions of Government Code §87100.

3. Job Training Plan.

Each SDA and its affiliated PIC are required to enter into "an agreement or
agreements" to develop a job training plan for the SDA and to select a grant recipient to receive
JTPA funds and a grant administrator to administer the plan. 29 U.S.C. §1513. Pursuant to
these provisions, SETA has a formal written agreement with PIC, which provides that SETA
and PIC shall jointly develop appropriate job training plans and that SETA shall serve as both
the local grant recipient of JTPA funds and as the local administrator to administer the job
training plan.

Each job training plan is approved independently by SETA and PIC and submitted
jointly by them to the appropriate state officials. 29 U.S.C. §1514. Each job training plan
developed under JTPA is a comprehensive document that contains a two-year program budget
and procedures for identifying and selecting participants, performance goals, a description of
services to be provided under the plan, and "procedures for selecting service providers which
takes into account past performance in job training or related activities." 29 U.S.C. §1514.

4. Selecting Service Providers.

In selecting service providers within the local SDA, SETA and PIC have
historically utilized a Request for Proposal (RFP) process whereby potential service providers

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 13, 1992
Page 3

(Program Operators) respond to an RFP to deliver JTPA services to the community. Program
Operators are, generally, community-based/non-profit organizations, private for-profit entities,
school districts or other local government entities. In addition, SETA itself operates some
programs.

Each RFP is initially prepared by SETA staff and then submitted to the PIC for
approval or modification. Once the PIC has approved the RFP, it is then submitted to the SETA
Board for concurrence.

Once the RFP has been approved by the PIC and the Board has concurred in such
approval, it is formally circulated to the public for response. Interested Program Operators
submit formal responses to each RFP. After all responses are received and reviewed, the PIC
conducts a series of hearings whereby Program Operators provide answers to any questions
concerning their individual proposals and, ultimately, the PIC determines which Program
Operators it believes should receive funds and the amount of the funds to be allocated. After
the PIC reaches its decision, the list of Program Operators is submitted to the Board for
consideration and concurrence in the selection of Program Operators. Should the PIC and the
Board not agree, a conference committee of the PIC and the Board, comprised of equal numbers
from each group (generally two PIC members and two Board members), meets and confers until
its members can agree on an allocation of funds and selection of Program Operators. The
agreed upon selection and allocation is then submitted to the entire PIC for review and approval.
Upon such approval, it is then submitted to the Board for final concurrence. Once the successful
Program Operators have been selected, the selected Program Operators then enter into subgrants
with SETA whereby they deliver the actual services.

Historically, the RFP process is repeated every year or every two years. Program
Operators who maintain programs with demonstrated success are ofttimes able to successfully
compete for funds in subsequent years because of their "past performance in job training or
related activities." The Board and the PIC must, however, determine not only which entities
receive funding in a given funding cycle, but how the funds will be allocated among the selected
Program Operators. Thus, members of the Board and members of PIC are constantly required
to assess programs and vote on how JTPA funds will be distributed to Program Operators. In
situations where numerous potential Program Operators have solicited funds, each member of
the Board and the PIC is required to individually decide how to vote on the issue of funding for
each Program Operator.

5. On-the-Job Training.

One of the components of the JTPA program is On-the-Job Training (OJT). OJT
is a program pursuant to which Program Operators enter into formal, written "OJT Contracts"
that provide for the Program Operator to pay an employer for up to one-half of the salary of an

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 13, 1992
Page 4

OJT participant¹ for a period not to exceed six months. The employer agrees to provide the
participant with on-the-job-training in exchange for the salary assistance. The contract process,
therefore, permits an employer to hire an untrained employee, rather than a trained employee,
thereby allowing the participant to be trained for, and hopefully obtain, permanent employment
with the employer. Theoretically, the program does not provide a financial advantage to the
employer, since the employer receives salary assistance in exchange for hiring an untrained
individual.

In the funding process, the Board and the PIC allocate a portion of JTPA funds
to Program Operators for OJT services. After this allocation of funding is completed, the
individual Program Operator contracts with interested employers for actual delivery of OJT
services.

B. Potential Conflicts of Interest.

To assist in oversight and assessment of the OJT program, SETA and PIC have adopted
a formal OJT Manual. This manual contains guidelines and procedures for administering the
program and identifies various provisions that must be contained in all OJT contracts. Current
restrictions of the SETA OJT Manual, as well as specific limitations in the OJT contract,
prohibit employers from entering into OJT contracts if any member of the PIC is an officer,
director or employee of the employer. These restrictions exceed the requirements of the JTPA
regarding conflict of interest, which merely provide that "[n]o member of any [private industry]
council under this Act shall cast a vote on the provision of services by that member (or any
organization which that member directly represents) or vote on any matter which would provide
direct financial benefit to that member." 29 U.S.C. §1551(f). Historically, SETA's more
restrictive provisions were developed because of the prohibitions contained in Government Code
§1090 and general concerns of the Board regarding conflicts of interest and appearances of
conflict of interest. Due to the legislative action in 1985, the Section 1090 restrictions no longer
exist, provided that the individal PIC members comply with Section 87100.

Recently, members of the PIC have inquired into the legality and feasibility of
eliminating these contract restrictions and permitting employers to enter into OJT contracts with
Program Operators and/or the SETA in-house program, notwithstanding the fact that a PIC
member is an officer, director or employee of the contracting employer. Many members of the
PIC have indicated that, if permitted to do so, their employer or company would be very
interested in participating in the OJT contracting process. Since finding willing OJT employers
is always a challenge, permitting such contracts could help the agency further its goal of finding
appropriate employment for its participants. Moreover, it would permit PIC members to serve
as examples of how an OJT Program can be effectively utilized.

¹ "Participants" are individuals who apply for and qualify to receive program benefits and are enrolled
in a program. In the OJT program, participants would literally become employees of the contracting employer.

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 13, 1992
Page 5

As you know, the Political Reform Act provides that "[n]o public official (which includes
members of PIC) shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has
a financial interest." Government Code §87100. Considering the definition of financial interest
contained in Government Code §87103, we are uncertain as to whether PIC members might have
a financial interest if the members employer enters into an OJT contract.

Assuming that such a financial interest does exist, further questions are presented
regarding the procedure that a PIC member must follow in order to comply with the Political
Reform Act during the funding process required by the JTPA. The RFP funding process
envisions significant involvement of PIC members in the process of Job Training Plan
development, RFP preparation and, ultimately, Program Operator selection. In situations where
a number of potential Program Operators have applied for funding pursuant to the RFP, we are
concerned that individual PIC members may have a conflict of interest requiring disclosure and
abstention under the Political Reform Act. Although abstaining from voting on the direct award
of funds to any Program Operator with which the member or the member's employer has
contracted, the individual member will still be participating in the funding process and voting
in favor of, or against, competing proposals. In this instance, even though the PIC member has
not directly participated in the vote to award funds to that Program Operator, the member may
have indirectly participated in such an award by precluding the award from being made to other
Program Operators.

We are concerned that, because of this latter possibility, the individual PIC member
might be required to abstain from participation in the entire funding process, as opposed to just
abstaining from participation in the actual vote in favor of funding for the Program Operator
with whom he has a conflict. If that is so, the entire funding process could be very difficult for
PIC to process, especially if several different PIC members have OJT contracts with Program
Operators.

Questions Presented.

Within the framework of the background information provided herein, the Board solicits
a formal FPPC opinion regarding the following questions:

1. Does a PIC member have a financial interest under the Political Reform Act that
requires the member to abstain from participating in JTPA funding decisions
when the member or the member's employer has entered into an OJT contract
with a Program Operator which is soliciting funds?

2. What must a PIC member do to comply with the Political Reform Act when
voting on JTPA funding decisions when the member or the member's employer
has entered into an OJT contract with a Program Operator which is soliciting
funds? Specifically:

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 13, 1992
Page 6

A.      Would it be permissible for a PIC member to abstain from voting only on
awards of JTPA funds to Program Operators with which the member or
the member's employer has had a contract?;

B.      Would the PIC member be required to abstain from voting on awards of
JTPA funds to all Program Operators if the member or the member's
employer has contracted with any of the Program Operators?; and,

C.      Would the PIC member be required to abstain from participating in all
discussions of RFP's if the member or the member's employer has
contracted with any of the Program Operators responding to the RFP at
issue?

We trust that the background facts provided herein sufficiently explain the concerns raised
by the Board. If additional information is necessary please do not hesitate to contact us.

Very truly yours,

GREGORY D. THATCH
Attorney for Sacramento
Employment and Training Agency

GDT/ca
L3822.ltr
enclosure

sections.question_synthetic: Whether members of the Private Industry Council (PIC) may participate in voting on JTPA procurement activities and fund allocation decisions when their employers are interested in contracting as on-the-job training (OJT) providers, and what procedures should apply to manage conflicts of interest under Government Code Section 87100.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from legal counsel, not an FPPC response. The document is truncated mid-text. Contains extensive background facts and regulatory context but no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections visible. The legal question is synthesized from the letter's stated purpose in the opening paragraph and discussion of conflicts of interest issues.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether members of the Private Industry Council (PIC) may participate in voting on JTPA procurement activities and fund allocation decisions when their employers are interested in contracting as on-the-job training (OJT) providers, and what procedures should apply to manage conflicts of interest under Government Code Section 87100.

embedding.summary: A law firm representing the Sacramento Employment and Training Agency seeks FPPC guidance on conflict of interest procedures for PIC members voting on JTPA fund allocation and procurement decisions, specifically regarding whether PIC members whose employers wish to participate in OJT contracting may participate in related voting decisions.
